records.transcriptText: Don't be this guy. I meet these people all the time. The guy who is honestly rich inside of their business, but weirdly unclear outside of it, at least financially speaking. The reason why this is a problem is when you have a business there are two things that's successful. There are two things that make you think you're doing really well. Your net worth, you can, a lot of people ego inflate what their business is really worth, number one. And number two, there's a lot of cashflow coming in. So they feel like they can buy things. And a lot of entrepreneurs are first-generation wealthy and so they feel like it's more than they've had in the past. The problem is that is more fragile than most people take at face value. So if all of your eggs are in this one basket of a business, like of course that's a good way to create wealth. That's one of, I mean, probably the main way how wealth is created in America. But it isn't real financial independence, the type of independence that has like this deep confidence. Like if you know somebody who's like really wealthy and they have like this centered confidence when it comes to anything about money, that is essentially peace. And not everyone should have that ambition, but most people starting a business that is part of the ambition. And so they kind of have this like smokescreen and like they don't actually go the final step to get there. The way that you get there is through the traditional financial planning. Now it does help to work with somebody who speaks business. You know, that's a big deal, but tax planning work, estate planning work, cashflow management, setting up investment accounts, like buying real estate, like doing other things than just your business helps you get there. And you don't wanna be that guy who has this false kind of ego front where they're rich inside the business, but outside of it, they're just like confused, but they don't admit it to anyone other than themselves occasionally. I don't know. It's a circle that I wish more people would try to fix.

records.transcriptText: the tax strategies you had in the early years may have not grown up with you now that you're a successful business owner. And that's normal. It works that way and everything and yet taxes are an easy thing to procrastinate until you talk about them once a year. And so they take many more years of like lag time before your tax strategies often catch up with where they should be based on your income and your business success. And that is like, I mean, it's so often that I meet with like tens of thousands of dollars a year, sometimes hundreds of thousands of dollars a year that I mean, that's not uncommon at all. Like a few times a week, I meet with somebody and it's $100,000 of tax savings because of a conversation that they meant to have four years ago. So don't do that. If you have a hunch that there are people making the same amount of money that you have, like if you have whoever you see as a peer financially, as an other business owner or whatever, like if you have a hunch that there are people that are paying less taxes than you at your level of financial success or business success, then you're probably right. Lean into that. Like there's a good chance that you're right. And you don't have to understand everything about the tax code. The one good thing about tax code is it's mostly logical. Like if you're halfway intelligent and you have just a little bit of time with a tax strategist, like you can grasp these things. It isn't that hard, but you have to take the time to do it. And the key is that the entire playbook changes. If you use a sports analogy, like the play calls when you're in high school playing football are different than the play calls in NFL or when there's an eight-year-old playing. Like the entire thing is completely different. And it's the same thing with tax strategy as your income goes up. Like it isn't that you wasted time learning the foundational things, but the whole strategy changes and you need to keep up with that because taxes are dollars too. And most people would rather have them. If you would rather have your dollars than pay them to the IRS, like 99% of Americans would probably say that. One way or the other. Maybe I'm overstating that. Maybe it's not 99, but everybody that I talk to, at least it seems, would rather keep their money than pay it to the IRS. Even if they keep it and give it away, they would rather choose where it is given to than give it to the IRS. But point is those are dollars too. And by doing tax planning as you get more successful, what that means is you have to upgrade the playbook. You have to understand different types of strategies. The game gets faster. The game gets bigger. You need to learn how to keep up.

records.transcriptText: if most of your tax conversation happens in April or whenever it is that you file your taxes, it's a pretty good bet that you're missing stuff. Like you're probably paying more in taxes than you could be paying. The way I phrase it is you're tipping the IRS. And it isn't that like filing your taxes is a necessary and good thing. I run a tax firm. I file taxes. But the point is most tax work should happen in the planning stages. It should happen in things like how you pay yourself as a business owner, what your estimates or quarterly payments are, tax withholding, your charitable giving, your equipment purchases. I mean like there's probably 75 things that should be planned. You don't make decisions solely because of the tax implication. The taxes are a component of the decision. And throughout the year, like once usually we do tax strategy sessions shortly after tax season. So like this time of year may and then we'll do another one towards the end of the fall, maybe like October, November when you have a good clear picture for the end of the year. And the intent there is so that that like we're, I hate the word proactive because it's kind of eye rolly. It's like synergize or something like that. But proactive is the right word. Like you have to make tax decisions in advance. You have to be forward looking. You have to decide like I'm going to do something today that is better for me tomorrow. And if you're making tax decisions from the perspective of like, oh, last year, whoops, what's the, what's the damage, then like you're, you are paying more than you have to pay. And so keep that in mind, take action on it, start doing things now, learning strategies now that will make you have more cash next April.

bestSourceLines.label: Organizing thesis
bestSourceLines.text: Most tax savings for business owners and individuals come from decisions made before year-end—not at filing. If your only tax conversation happens at tax time, you're likely paying more than you need to.

bestSourceLines.label: Deeper problem
bestSourceLines.text: People treat taxes as a filing event instead of a year-round series of decisions. This leads to missed strategies and unnecessary tax payments because tax-reducing actions have to be taken before the year ends.

bestSourceLines.label: Surface question

bestSourceLines.label: short caption

bestSourceLines.label: thread opener

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 1

records.transcriptText: A lot of business owners say they want to sell their business someday, they have no idea when that's going to be, and so they don't do any work in pursuit of setting up the business so that it can be sold. But here's the crazy thing, most, I have an inside look at this because I do a lot of succession planning where businesses like, hey in two to three years you want to sell my business, and so they do all this work to make the business worth more. So make it a business that someone would want to buy. And what happens is it's more fun to run when they do that work. And then when it comes time to sell it, they don't even really want to sell it because they have set up things in a way that it's a better business to run. And so a business that is ready to be sold is a business that you don't want to sell. And so I love the thought of running a business like it's always about to be sold. Now it's not completely right because there are some things like when you're about to sell a business like how do you set up your compensation, some like the operational things, yes, you should act like you're about to sell it within the next few years. The financial things, not always, like the way you do some of your accounting should maybe change. The way you decide on certain types of customers should change if you're going to sell your business just to maximize the amount that you're going to sell it for. But the operational type things like who does what and when and the systems like all of that if you set it up to where someone else could come in and take over like all of a sudden you can step out and you don't have to do all the work and like that's a fun business to run. And so the mindset of more people, more entrepreneurs should be I want to run this thing like I'm going to sell it in a couple years and I need to set it up as if that's the case. Plus it gives you this like a newfound sense of financial security because you have a business that is actually sellable and that's also nice.

records.transcriptText: how you pay yourself as a business owner is such an important decision for so many reasons. The types of tax deductions that are available to you, how much tax you actually pay, regardless of other strategies that you use, how much the business is worth when you sell it. And the thing about the pay decisions, like how to take an owner draw, they have impacts on like, like in order to make that decision, well, you have to dive into what tax, what tax, you know, how much income you're going to have, what entity type do you want to be, when might you sell the business, what other income is in place, what types of retirement plans your accounts are already set up versus you might set up in the future. It's, it's like you kind of spin in a circle almost when you're making those decisions. So you need space for discussion to decide those things. But the key here is that when you're deciding how much to pay yourself, if you're not intentional with that, then there, and you just take draws as you need them, or you just do what you have done for years and you haven't looked at it freshly and made any changes, then the safe assumption is that you're probably leaving stuff on the table just because you're not being intentional. And that's a shame. We should be intentional about that stuff. So if you ever have, you haven't ever really had a deep dive and you're a business owner, how do I pay myself? How much, what's the structure? Is it W-2? Is it 1099? Do I just take owner draws? Like, what does that really even mean? If you don't know that stuff, then you should. And if it's been a few years since you've taken a look at it and your income has changed, then you should, because there are all these like thresholds where if you get above a certain level of net income and you're above a certain level of gross revenue or, you know, in like they kind of move against one another, then you should be one entity type versus another. And the entity type structure is how you pay yourself and when you pay yourself and how you pay quarterly estimates or, you know, like it all cycles around each other. So that's a word salad, everything that I just said. But the point is, if you don't understand how that works cleanly, then you should, because you're probably leaving money on the table if you don't.

records.transcriptText: Look, as an entrepreneur, I can say this too, if you run a business, like we all want our businesses to become wildly successful. And that's good. What happens though, is that often the business starts having too many jobs. It's your legacy, it's your cash flow and your income and your immediate security and your midterm security and your retirement plan and your purpose in life. It's your life, it's your baby, it's your passion, it's your hobby. It's like all of these things rolled up into one and that is too much weight on a singular thing. It's just like, I don't want to go into therapist mode, but like that is too much weight. Like that, like, what if it fails? Like that is possible. Like, I mean, it might not even be in your control. Like I might wipe your business out. I don't know. Like the point is that you should start at least financially speaking, and I can speak to that with authority. You should start diversifying some of that, it's too much of a used word, but like you should start moving some of the weight of your financial responsibility into other things. If your business is this everything and so many business owners, if it weren't everything for them, then it would surely fail because being an entrepreneur is an insane idea in the first place. It should never work. And yet sometimes it does. And so you need that kind of ownership, like responsibility, not ownership in like the legal sense, but ownership and like, this is on my back. I'm going to do this thing. I'm going to make it there. And so you need some level of that. When you start having so much success, that it's also your, your full retirement plan and your full, you know, legacy after you passed away, you're going to pass these dollars to your kids. And you're like, just all of that stuff, like you got to start spreading things out. You need another hobby. You need to start investing money outside of your business. You need to like do other things. And so, um, public service announcement, I guess for all the other entrepreneurs out there, I run into this every day, both personally, I guess, but also in business owners that I work with, um, that too much folks in a singular thing is fine for a little while, but over a long period of time, it's not, once you get more successful, you have just gained freedom to not do that. Take advantage of it.

records.transcriptText: So you heard Roth conversions are smart to do. And so you started doing them a little bit each year. But most of the time, when I meet with somebody, the question or the problem wasn't that they weren't doing Roth conversions at all, although that certainly happens. It's that they weren't doing them in a meaningful enough amount that it actually makes a difference anyway. And so, yeah, it was worth doing, but it's like, just if you're gonna do it, do it and take care of the risks or offset the risks that you wanted to do them in the first place. If you read an article or you listen to a podcast or watch a video like mine that says like, hey, do Roth conversions. There's all these reasons why you might be taxed at a higher rate down the road than you would be right now if you did them a little bit each year. But most people underdo that. Or at the very least, they're not precise enough about it. They don't fill up the tax bracket or they don't fill up to a strategic point like the super senior standard deduction or the IRMA threshold or whatever it is. Just don't need to get into the details. The point is so many people do Roth conversions, but they don't do them in a meaningful enough amount to actually be worth doing the strategy in the first place. And so they think they're checking the box, but they haven't mapped things out long enough to really see the weight of what they're doing. And so they should have doubled the amount or tripled the amount or whatever it is that actually makes it meaningful.

bestSourceLines.label: Organizing thesis
bestSourceLines.text: A Roth conversion strategy is only effective if the amount and the window are mapped out with enough precision to actually make a difference. Checking the box isn’t the same as shifting risk or tax exposure in a meaningful way.

bestSourceLines.label: Deeper problem
bestSourceLines.text: Most people underdo Roth conversions, believing they're taking action, but they're not making a strategic difference. The root problem is lack of precision and not mapping out the long-term cumulative effect.

bestSourceLines.label: Surface question
bestSourceLines.text: Should I be doing Roth conversions each year?

bestSourceLines.label: short caption
bestSourceLines.text: Most people don’t do enough Roth conversion for it to matter.

bestSourceLines.label: thread opener
bestSourceLines.text: Doing a little Roth conversion each year might feel proactive, but often it doesn’t make a strategic difference.

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 1

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 2
bestSourceLines.text: “Checking the Roth conversion box is not the same as actual planning.”

records.transcriptText: So you inherited money, and you feel this almost guilt about it. I've seen that a lot of times with people is like, they got $100,000 or $10 million, whatever it is. And they're like, like, I didn't like what I don't, I don't know how to handle this emotionally. And so they just kind of pause and they don't do much now they might invest some of it. But the reality is when you when you inherit money, you have like that is legally yours now it like the entire tax law set up to pretty much allow you to reset things. There are exceptions, but you can reset things in your best interest. And you have an obligation if the person cared about you who who, who gave you the money or you know, you know, put you as their beneficiary on all their stuff like they want you to act in their best interest the vast majority of the time and people will hold on to whatever the parent like if the parents had CDs, they'll keep the money in CDs if they know whatever, you know, if they had an investment in an IRA, they'll just keep the same investment. And it's very likely that a saving 87 year old who passed away doesn't have the same investment objectives or risk tolerance or capacity to understand complexity or whatever that you do. And so like, you really do have an obligation to to like, treat it as it is now yours because it is and if I don't tell you that no one might tell you that and so if you inherit money, you have inherited money like it's yours and you need to deal with it with honor and respect and that means treat it as real

bestSourceLines.label: Organizing thesis
bestSourceLines.text: When you inherit money, it is legally and practically yours. You have a responsibility to treat it with respect—by making decisions that fit your life, not just mirroring what the previous owner did.

bestSourceLines.label: Deeper problem
bestSourceLines.text: People can feel guilty or unsure about inheriting money and often freeze or do nothing, treating the inheritance as if it still belongs to the person who passed away.

bestSourceLines.label: Surface question
bestSourceLines.text: What should I do with money I just inherited?

bestSourceLines.label: short caption
bestSourceLines.text: If you inherit money, it’s really yours. You honor the gift by making the right decisions for your own life.

bestSourceLines.label: thread opener
bestSourceLines.text: Most people freeze when they inherit money. They leave investments as-is, almost as if they're keeping it on hold for the person who left it.

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 1
bestSourceLines.text: If your approach to inherited money is to keep everything as it was, ask yourself: would you do the same with any other windfall?

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 2
bestSourceLines.text: You don’t honor someone’s legacy by pausing your own life. You honor it by making considered decisions that serve you now.

bestSourceLines.label: Organizing thesis
bestSourceLines.text: Without a real plan that shows you how much is enough and when you can let up, it’s hard to ever believe you’ve arrived. Hope comes from seeing an endpoint, not just from working harder.

bestSourceLines.label: Deeper problem

bestSourceLines.label: Surface question
bestSourceLines.text: Is all the stress and hard work really worth it if you don’t know when it ends?

bestSourceLines.label: short caption

bestSourceLines.label: thread opener
bestSourceLines.text: A lot of high earners come to me stressed and burned out, even with incomes most would envy.

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 1

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 2

records.transcriptText: David, what's the worst question you're ever asked? It's probably, what rate of return should I have on my investments? That is at least four layers more basic than what a good question should be. Like I could kind of peel back each one of those before we arrive at an actually better question. Now, once you understand those other layers to the question, then that's a fair question. But it's almost always not the case. And I'm going a little bit of a rant here because it is just such a big deal that people grasp this. When you're choosing how to invest your money and what it should be invested in, there are literally tens of millions, hundreds of millions of investment options out there. And there are two filters you always have to go through in order to narrow that down. And then you can start to have the conversation about what rate of return should I have. So first filter is how long do these dollars have to live before you're going to pull them out? When you get close to retirement, that's nuanced because some of the dollars you're going to distribute like next year, some of the dollars have seven years to live, some of them might have 30, so there are stages there. But that's the first one and people forget that. But the second one, and this one is a big deal, I think of these as filters to go through, is how much volatility can you endure without freaking out and making a mistake and calling me or your advisor or whatever and like, hey, David, I like you, I really do. Like I want to trust you, but I'm freaked out like this swing is too much. Some people, if their account goes down a dollar, they might actually have a stroke. Some people can go down 80% and it's fine. They just want a higher average long term rate of return. And so you have to go through those two filters. And once you do, then you can narrow down the world of investment options for a portion of your portfolio, you can start having a conversation about how much return you should actually have. Now, it's usually always still a bad question, because it's not really how much rate of return should I get? It's what should my average rate of return be? And what level of volatility is normal? So pick like SP 500 index fund is very normal to have a 30% up year or 30% down year, roughly I'm generalizing. But like, if you're targeting, I'm going to get 10% yield that actually almost never happens. I think I saw a stat on this that the, you know, the average rate of return SP 500 reinvested dividends like 10 and a half percent for the past 110 years or some timeframe similar to that. But that you it's only actually earned between 10 and 11% like five times in 110 years. So that actual rate of return almost never happens. It's all over the place. And so it's so important that you don't look at it like what rate of return am I getting? You look at is are these assets that I should own is the portfolio diversified, which means there's tension, some things are up, some things are down on purpose. And is the rate of return normal based on what's going on in the world and the amount of risk that I'm able to endure and the goal that these dollars are attributed to? That's the right thinking. And when you're choosing the type of investments that you should own. And if you don't follow all of that in too much detail, like talk to a financial planner, help them educate you more than a three minute video. But the first terrible question remains. And if you feel like it's a good question, then you need to like watch this video again, like dig into this deeper. Because the question isn't what rate of return should I have next year? It is a lot bigger than that. So hope that helps.

bestSourceLines.label: Organizing thesis
bestSourceLines.text: The right investment decisions start with knowing your time horizon and your real tolerance for volatility—then deciding what job your money needs to do. The rate of return is an output, not a target. Framing the question this way may lead to better portfolio decisions.

bestSourceLines.label: Deeper problem
bestSourceLines.text: Most people are asking for a single number without understanding the bigger sequence of decisions that determines what return is even relevant, or what their plan and risk profile require.

bestSourceLines.label: Surface question
bestSourceLines.text: What rate of return should I have on my investments?

bestSourceLines.label: short caption
bestSourceLines.text: Don’t start with rate of return. Start with time horizon and how much swing you can handle.

bestSourceLines.label: thread opener
bestSourceLines.text: The most common question I get is, 'What rate of return should I look for?' It sounds simple, but skips key steps.

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 1
bestSourceLines.text: If you start your investment plan by asking about return targets, you skip the work that actually matters.

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 2
bestSourceLines.text: "First, decide what job each dollar needs to do before worrying about the return." , David Talley

records.transcriptText: I just had a meeting with somebody who's about to retire, they're an executive, they make a couple million dollars a year and their life's work is this business that they have grown over the years and they were hiring me, assumptively, to figure out, you know, can I retire, how do taxes work, how should my investments be managed, I need my state funding done, all the things, but I ask them, hey, when you retire, I think it's like September, what are you going to actually do with your time, like it's Monday or Tuesday, like what are you going to do, like, well, I've got a lot of list of things to do and I presented that a little bit of like what, you know, like how much things do you really have and it hit them and we spent like 90 minutes just talking about their list of like what they want their life to be, they almost cried, it was just a, it was actually beautiful, I feel like a therapist even though I'm not, we didn't talk about anything other than like what they want their life to be and you could tell it was cathartic for them to just let that out, they'd never really thought through it, what happens most of the time is that people either aren't asked that question, that's most of the time, or they are asked and they shrug it off and they don't really get to the heart of it or maybe even worse, they try but they can't because they don't have anything beyond their career and those are all real problems when it comes to retirement planning, when we're talking about retirement planning, we talk about the math and the investments and the taxes and like that stuff matters, yes, of course, we need to make sure that you're going to be sound and safe financially but what are you actually going to do, people need to think about that.

bestSourceLines.label: Organizing thesis
bestSourceLines.text: True retirement readiness is not just about financial security. It requires asking what life will look like when the career ends, and many people have never been asked—or answered—that question.

bestSourceLines.label: Deeper problem
bestSourceLines.text: Many people reach retirement age without thinking deeply about what their life will actually look like or what will give them purpose and satisfaction beyond work.

bestSourceLines.label: Surface question
bestSourceLines.text: Can I retire, and how do the numbers work?

bestSourceLines.label: short caption
bestSourceLines.text: Retirement planning is more than just numbers. What do you want life to be after work?

bestSourceLines.label: thread opener
bestSourceLines.text: Most retirement planning is about the numbers. But almost no one stops to ask: what do you actually want your life to be after work?

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 1
bestSourceLines.text: Retirement is the first time many people realize they never planned for purpose. Numbers are the easy part. Monday is the hard part.

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 2
bestSourceLines.text: The biggest risk in retirement may not be running out of money. It’s running out of meaning.

bestSourceLines.label: Organizing thesis
bestSourceLines.text: For some businesses already, the real work doesn't start with opening apps or browsers anymore. It starts with natural, conversational back-and-forth with AI systems that handle the tasks in the background. That shift is here sooner than most realize.

bestSourceLines.label: Deeper problem
bestSourceLines.text: Many people still imagine AI as a novelty or just a helper, but the way work happens—how decisions, creation, and coordination actually unfold—is changing fast for people willing to adapt. There's friction for those who don't realize how second-nature this can become.

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 1

records.transcriptText: Part two, why your tax strategy needs to grow with your business. A lot of families run into this. The idea here, I'll give two examples. One is your entity structure. There's often this area in Tennessee, at least is how it works because the franchise and excess tax were below a certain revenue number. It doesn't make sense to be an S corporation. Then there is a range, once you reach a certain net income number, there's a couple hundred thousand dollar range in the middle there where it does make sense to be an S corporation. Then after you get above that number, it no longer makes sense to. Most people go back to a partnership. The speed at which their business might accelerate through that middle range, or it might be a business where they're probably going to happily be in that middle range for a long period of time, depending on growth ambitions and goals. Also an example of how the plan of what you want out of your life and your business should dictate your strategy. In this example, your entity type in Tennessee, that you outgrow your old business and then you once again outgrow your old business entity type. Another example is the retirement plan design for a small business or closely held business. This is a complex web of just like overlapping stuff. There's simple IRAs, SEP IRAs, solo 401ks, a traditional type of safe harbor 401k plan, cash balance plans. As your income goes up, different versions of these kick in to where almost think of them as you add just a little bit of cost and administrative complexity in return for more cap space, more space that you can put more money in. If it's reasonable to assume you're going to put more money in, then you want to create that cap space because the added tax advantage, whether you're raw or traditional, the advantages of having those investment dollars in the tax advantage space instead of a taxable non-qualified account is worth a little bit of administrative or fee that might come with having a plan administrator or something like that. But if you're maybe or maybe not, it might not be worth it to level up to the next plan. There's several versions of that that depend on age, depend on your employees, what your employees do, what your profit is, all kinds of stuff that might dictate what your retirement plan is. Another example might be the level of purchasing the building that your business has run out of for a cost segregation and bonus depreciation. It's very reasonable. I've done 30 of these in the past year, probably where a business buys their location and they get a $300,000 deduction that year because of a cost segregation and bonus depreciation on it where they were paying rent and $300,000 that their tax bracket saved them $130,000. That's a good deal. And so there's most of the purchase price is back in tax savings in their examples. It's not a promise obviously, depends on the situation, but you get the idea. There are many different thresholds and levels where growth introduces a new tax world, a new playground that you get to go up to the big kid playground now. And there's always a bigger kid playground, but that's the analogy that I would go with.

bestSourceLines.label: Organizing thesis
bestSourceLines.text: As your business grows, several planning thresholds—entity choice, retirement plan options, and building ownership—require you to reconsider your tax strategy so you do not leave money or opportunity on the table. The smartest move is letting your plan lead the tax and entity decisions, not the other way around.

bestSourceLines.label: Deeper problem
bestSourceLines.text: Most owners never revisit their entity type or tax approach as profits change, missing important thresholds that would affect both their taxes and long-term wealth. Retirement plan designs, real estate purchases, and entity choices all have inflection points that are easy to miss in the middle of day-to-day business.

bestSourceLines.label: Surface question
bestSourceLines.text: When should I update my tax strategy or entity structure as my business grows?

bestSourceLines.label: short caption
bestSourceLines.text: You outgrow more than just your revenue as a business owner—your plan should grow too.

bestSourceLines.label: thread opener
bestSourceLines.text: Most business owners outgrow their first entity type, retirement plan, or tax setup. It rarely happens all at once, it’s gradual, but the thresholds matter.

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 1
bestSourceLines.text: Your CPA can file last year’s return, but growth means rethinking if last year’s tax strategy still fits. That is a planning question, not just a math question.

bestSourceLines.label: standalone 2
bestSourceLines.text: If your business profits change meaningfully, so will the right answers about your entity and retirement plan. Old decisions can quietly become outdated.
